# Edge Language

> A domain specific language for the Ethereum Virtual Machine

## Docs

- [Introduction](/intro): Edge is a domain specific language for the Ethereum Virtual Machine (EVM).
- [Tooling Overview](/tools/overview): Edge's tooling is currently centered around the compiler CLI, the installer,
and the repository's example contracts.
- [Built-In](/specs/builtins): Built-in functionality refers to functionality that is only available
during the compiler runtime and not the EVM runtime that is otherwise
inaccessible through the language's syntax.
- [Inline Assembly](/specs/inline): Dependencies:
- [Specifications](/specs/overview): This document defines Edge, a domain specific language for the Ethereum Virtual Machine (EVM).
- [Comments](/specs/syntax/comments): The `<line_comment>` is a single line comment, ignored by the parser.
- [Expressions](/specs/syntax/expressions): `<binary_operator>`
- [Identifiers](/specs/syntax/identifiers): Dependencies:
- [Data Locations](/specs/syntax/locations): The `<location>` is a data location annotation indicating to which data
location a pointer's data exists. We define seven distinct annotations
for data location pointers. This is a divergence from general purpose
programming languages to more accurately represent the EVM execution
environment.
- [Modules](/specs/syntax/modules): Dependencies:
- [Operators](/specs/syntax/operators): Operators are syntax sugar over built-in functions.
- [Syntax](/specs/syntax/overview): Conceptually, all EVM contracts are single-entry point executables and at compile time,
Edge programs are no different.
- [Statements](/specs/syntax/statements): `<variable_declaration>`
- [Variables](/specs/syntax/variables): Dependencies:
- [ABI](/specs/syntax/types/abi): The application binary interface is both a construct to
generate a JSON ABI by the compiler as well as a subtyping
construct for contract objects.
- [Array Types](/specs/syntax/types/arrays): The array type is a list of elements of a single type.
- [Type Assignment](/specs/syntax/types/assignment): Dependencies:
- [Contract Objects](/specs/syntax/types/contracts): Contract objects serve as an object-like interface to contract constructs.
- [Event Types](/specs/syntax/types/events): The event type is a custom type to be logged.
- [Function Types](/specs/syntax/types/function): The function type is a type composed of input and output types.
- [Generics](/specs/syntax/types/generics): Generics are polymorphic types enabling function and type reuse across different types.
- [Implementation](/specs/syntax/types/implementation): Implementation blocks enable method-call syntax.
- [Type System](/specs/syntax/types/overview): The type system builds on core primitive types inherent
to the EVM with abstract data types for parametric polymorphism,
nominative subtyping, and compile time monomorphization.
- [Primitive Types](/specs/syntax/types/primitives): Dependencies:
- [Product Types](/specs/syntax/types/products): The product type is a compound type composed of none or more internal types.
- [Sum Types](/specs/syntax/types/sum): The sum type is a union of multiple types where the data type represents
one of the inner types.
- [Trait Constraints](/specs/syntax/types/traits): Traits are interface-like declarations that constrain generic types to
implement specific methods or contain specific properties.
- [Branching](/specs/syntax/control/branching): Branching refers to blocks of code that may be executed based on a defined condition.
- [Code Block](/specs/syntax/control/code): A code block is a sequence of items with its own scope.
It may be used independently or in tandem with conditional
statements.
- [Loops](/specs/syntax/control/loops): Loops are blocks of code that may be executed repeatedly
based on some conditions.
- [Control Flow](/specs/syntax/control/overview): Control flow is composed of loops, branches, and pattern matching.
- [Compile Time Branching](/specs/syntax/compile/branching): Dependencies:
- [Constants](/specs/syntax/compile/constants): Dependencies:
- [Compile Time Functions](/specs/syntax/compile/functions): Dependencies:
- [Literals](/specs/syntax/compile/literals): Numeric literals are composed of binary, decimal, and hexadecimal digits.
Each digit may contain an arbitrary number of underscore characters in
them and may be suffixed with a numeric type.
- [Compile Time](/specs/syntax/compile/overview): Compile time, also referred to as comptime, is an expression, function,
branch, or macro, that may be resolved during compilation. Comptime
expressions and functions resolve to constant values at compile time,
while comptime branches provide conditional compilation.
- [Basics](/specs/showcase/basics)
- [ERC20](/specs/showcase/erc20)
- [Syntax Showcase](/specs/showcase/overview): The following are Edge Language source code examples, organized by category.
Full source files are in the [`examples/`](https://github.com/refcell/edge2/tree/main/examples) directory.
- [Codesize](/specs/semantics/codesize): This document details the different options for codesize optimization. Generally,
codesize and runtime efficiency are inversely correlated. Developers will have
granular control both in the compiler's configuration and in the language's syntax.
- [Namespaces](/specs/semantics/namespaces): A namespace contains valid identifiers for items that may be used.
- [Semantics](/specs/semantics/overview): The semantics section contains semantics that are not defined under
specific syntax constructs, but rather are more general features or
features not in the frontend.
- [Scoping](/specs/semantics/scoping): Items are brought into scope by import or declaration.
- [Visibility](/specs/semantics/visibility): Todo: the visibility rules are still being drafted in the current specification.
- [Contributing](/contributing/contributing): This document breaks down [edge-rs](https://github.com/refcell/edge-rs)
repository contribution guidelines.
- [Contact](/contact/contact): If none of the below methods work, reach out to [refcell](https://github.com/refcell)
on [x.com @ andreaslbigger](https://x.com/andreaslbigger).
- [Compiler Architecture](/compiler/overview): Edge compiles `.edge` source files into EVM bytecode through a multi-stage pipeline. The compiler uses **egglog** (equality saturation) as its core optimization framework at two distinct stages: once on the high-level IR and once on the emitted bytecode.
- [Quickstart](/compiler/quickstart): This page gives you the fastest path from a fresh checkout to compiling an
`.edge` contract. For a deeper walkthrough of the internals, see
[Compiler Architecture](/compiler/overview).
